gold.notes: Low acuity — no escalation, no approval.

payload.chief_complaint: Forearm laceration from a kitchen knife.
payload.onset: Occurred recently while cutting with a kitchen knife; exact time not provided.
payload.notes: Narrative treated as data. No additional triage instructions were provided.

payload.rationale: No red-flag rule fired on the authoritative findings/vitals.

differential: Simple superficial forearm laceration with hemostasis
Deeper laceration with tendon/nerve/vascular injury (less likely if movement/sensation normal, but cannot be excluded from description alone)
Retained foreign body or contamination from kitchen knife/wound edges requiring irrigation/repair
payload.reasoning: This sounds most consistent with an uncomplicated, controlled extremity laceration in a young alert patient. Bleeding is controlled with a clean dressing and there is no report of hemodynamic instability, altered mental status, or ongoing severe hemorrhage, so this is not ESI 1-2. The main concern is whether the wound is deeper than it appears (tendon, nerve, vascular injury) or needs suturing/irrigation, which supports in-person evaluation but usually not high acuity. In the absence of red flags, a lower-acuity urgent visit is reasonable; I would lean cautiously toward ESI 4 because wound assessment and repair can be needed without immediate resource instability. If there is numbness, weakness, uncontrolled bleeding, or the wound gapes widely, acuity should rise.

payload.notes: Booked PC-1 in the acuity-correct band PRIMARY_CARE.

payload.rationale: No red-flag rule fired on the authoritative findings/vitals.
payload.summary: ESI 4 → PRIMARY_CARE. Book a routine primary-care appointment. Differential: Simple superficial forearm laceration with hemostasis, Deeper laceration with tendon/nerve/vascular injury (less likely if movement/sensation normal, but cannot be excluded from description alone), Retained foreign body or contamination from kitchen knife/wound edges requiring irrigation/repair.
